bibliographic.citation: Hermann Varnhagen, ‘Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten. X. Zu den “Signa ante Judicium”,’ Anglia 3 (1880): 533-551.
bibliographic.title: Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten. X. Zu den ‘Signa ante Judicium’

entities.label: Varnhagen 1880 edition of two Middle English Fifteen Signs versions
notes: Printed page 533 contains the German preface; Cambridge text occupies pp. 534-542; the Cotton sequence occupies pp. 543-551; the closing signature and German addendum are on p. 551.
Varnhagen treats the two Cotton pieces continuously, while current DIMEV records them as distinct works 436 and 3000.

rights.basis: The texts and surviving manuscript witnesses are medieval and predate modern copyright by centuries.
rights.component: The three underlying anonymous medieval works and their medieval witness readings
notes: This conclusion does not govern modern photographs, facsimiles, scholarly editions, encodings, interfaces, or provider terms.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1880, far before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works.
rights.component: Varnhagen’s 1880 editorial text, introduction, notes, arrangement, and addendum

entities.description: The sole DIMEV witness of the 398-line work beginning ‘The grace of the Holy Ghost.’ The target now occupies ff. 50va-53ra; Varnhagen and the 1857 catalog use the earlier foliation ff. 42v-45r.

holding.notes: The public item-level description remains primarily in the 1857 printed catalog; CUL states that most medieval manuscripts are not yet represented by modern ArchiveSearch records.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38
notes: Historical 42v corresponds to current 50v, historical 43 to current 51, historical 44 to current 52, and historical 45r to current 53r.
The first four printed lines are part of DIMEV 5309, not a separate invocation work.

physicalDescription.extent: presently iii + 247 + i leaves, foliated 3-261 after rebinding and allowance for missing material
notes: The 1857 catalog described 247 leaves, double columns of about forty lines, a uniform mid-fifteenth-century hand, and missing leaves.
The Database of Middle English Romance reports two scribes and describes the codex as a broad religious, didactic, exemplary, and romance compilation.
DIMEV associates the target hand with LALME/eLALME LP 531, Grid 445 305, Leicestershire. This target-language localization is not a proven place of codex production.

rights.basis: CUL distinguishes private-study or noncommercial research supply from publication and commercial licensing; no item-specific license has been issued.
rights.component: Any images supplied through Cambridge University Library Imaging Services
notes: Obtain written terms before redistributing supplied images in the repository or using them in any commercial publication workflow.

notes: The 2025/26 list quotes JPEG scanning at £15.25 for the first image and £0.58 thereafter, and photographic TIFF at £27.70 for the first image and £7.25 thereafter; manuscripts cannot be assumed eligible for scanning, so these figures are not a Cotton quote.
Complex handling is listed at £58.19. BL’s January 2026 terms state a standard thirty-working-day service after payment and require written permission for copying or reproduction.

entities.description: The direct source of Varnhagen’s second edited sequence. DIMEV distinguishes a 56-line Invocation to the Creator at f. 89ra-rb (record 436) from a following 301-line Fifteen Signs work at ff. 89rb-91rb (record 3000), although the manuscript and Varnhagen present them continuously.
entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Caligula A II
notes: The current BL component record summarizes the Invocation at f. 89r and the Fifteen Signs at ff. 89r-91r; DIMEV gives the more exact ff. 89ra-rb and 89rb-91rb.
The XML/Varnhagen milestone 91v conflicts with the current catalog and DIMEV. The target is not extended into the Owayne Miles material that the BL catalog begins at f. 91v.

physicalDescription.extent: 210 folios in the present parent volume; the relevant Middle English component occupies ff. 3-139
notes: The parent catalog describes an intact volume in a 1957 British Museum binding, containing two principal items.
The component catalog dates ff. 3-139 to the second half of the fifteenth century and identifies England as origin.
DIMEV reports a small paraph before record 3000, Arabic numbers beside each of the fifteen days, and a large centered yellow-washed ‘Amen for charite’ at f. 91rb.

rights.basis: No public whole-manuscript image object or item-specific license was available. BL’s imaging terms prohibit copying or reproduction without written permission and separate publication guidance applies.

notes: Request explicit repository redistribution and commercial print-on-demand terms; digitisation supply alone is not publication permission.

entities.description: The version 1.0 Digital Index of Middle English Verse records the Cambridge 398-line work as 5309 / IMEV-NIMEV 3368; the Cotton invocation as 436 / IMEV-NIMEV 256; and the following Cotton and Digby Fifteen Signs tradition as 3000 / IMEV-NIMEV 1823.
entities.label: DIMEV records 5309, 436, and 3000
notes: Record 5309 has one witness, CUL Ff.2.38, current ff. 50va-53ra, 398 couplet lines, edited by Varnhagen pp. 534-542.
Record 436 has three witnesses; its Cotton version is 28 couplets at f. 89ra-rb and is treated in that manuscript as an introduction to record 3000.
Record 3000 has six active witnesses in version 1.0. Its Cotton version occupies ff. 89rb-91rb; its Digby version occupies ff. 120v-122v and loses seventy lines with the outer half of f. 121.

rights.basis: The versioned release includes a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 license.
rights.component: DIMEV version 1.0 data release

next_steps: Consult modern Fifteen Signs scholarship and all DIMEV witness editions.
Keep witness dates separate from composition dates and avoid deriving one abstract poem from Varnhagen’s article boundary.
notes: Varnhagen explicitly leaves priority between the Cotton and Digby-related forms undecided.
openQuestions.question: When were DIMEV 5309, 436, and 3000 composed, and what is the historical relationship among their Fifteen Signs versions?

next_steps: Inspect a current color facsimile of ff. 89r-91v.
Compare Varnhagen’s foliation and line placement against the present BL binding and cataloging.
notes: Until resolved, use DIMEV’s precise ff. 89rb-91rb for the target and do not absorb Owayne Miles at f. 91v.
openQuestions.question: Why does the XML/Varnhagen sequence mark 91v near the Cotton ending when the current BL catalog ends the Fifteen Signs on 91r and DIMEV gives 91rb?

next_steps: Ask CUL Special Collections and Imaging Services about complete existing photography, microfilm, or an internal surrogate.
Ask the relevant rights holder whether the 1979 facsimile may be digitized or licensed for research access.
openQuestions.question: Does CUL hold unpublished whole-codex photography or a digitized copy of the 1979 Ff.2.38 facsimile, and what reuse terms would apply?

next_steps: Request a shelfmark-specific quote for the complete object and separately identify target ff. 89ra-91rb.
Request explicit written terms for Git repository access, research use, self-publishing, and for-profit print-on-demand use before distributing any supplied files.
openQuestions.question: Can the British Library supply a complete color facsimile of Cotton Caligula A II, and would its written terms permit repository redistribution or commercial print-on-demand use?

next_steps: Consult LALME/eLALME, LAEME where applicable, and manuscript-specific linguistic scholarship.
Do not transfer Digby’s north-west Gloucestershire target-language localization or Worcestershire production to the Cotton text.
openQuestions.question: What is the most reliable dialect or text-language localization for the Cotton copies of DIMEV 436 and 3000?

next_steps: Resolve ‘Curs. Mundi 22493 ff.’ and ‘Lond. text 176’ against Varnhagen’s bibliography and the other sections of Anglia volume 3.
Add supporting edition entities only after the exact quoted source layers are confirmed.
openQuestions.question: Which exact editions or witnesses supplied the eight Middle English lines quoted in Varnhagen’s Cursor Mundi and Hending notes?

relations.objectLabel: Stengel 1871 edition and description of Digby 86

reviewNotes: The cataloging and lineage boundary is Varnhagen’s complete article, including its German preface, two edited witness sections, comparative note quotations, signature, and addendum; it is not one abstract medieval poem.

The first 74 direct Cotton lines form one XML LG but cross an abstract-work boundary: direct lines 1-56 are DIMEV 436 and direct lines 57-74 begin DIMEV 3000. The following 283-line LG continues DIMEV 3000.

The Cambridge foliation in Varnhagen and the 1857 catalog is historical. DIMEV’s current ff. 50va-53ra corresponds to historical ff. 42v-45r, a target-level offset of eight leaves after rebinding and refoliation.
Access and rights records apply only to the named entity or component. Public-domain medieval text does not make modern photographs, facsimiles, interfaces, encodings, or scholarship freely reusable.
Negative availability findings are date-bounded to 2026-07-13 and do not prove that no private, legacy, or unindexed reproduction exists.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item CME00100, a keyed encoding of Hermann Varnhagen’s complete 1880 Anglia article. Varnhagen prints one 398-line Fifteen Signs work from Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38 and, continuously under a single Cotton heading, two distinct DIMEV works from British Library Cotton MS Caligula A II: a 56-line Invocation to the Creator and a 301-line Fifteen Signs work. The Cotton Fifteen Signs is compared with Edmund Stengel’s 1871 Digby 86 edition; Stengel, not Digby 86 directly, is Varnhagen’s cited comparative source. Complete public scans of Anglia volume 3, Stengel’s book, and Bodleian MS Digby 86 are cached outside Git at whole-object scope and mapped to the relevant pages or folios. No verified public whole-manuscript facsimile was found for Ff.2.38 or Cotton Caligula A II.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English authors or compilers
catalogSummary.date: Medieval components attested from 1272-1282 to the mid-fifteenth century; edited selection first published 1880; composition dates unknown
catalogSummary.region: England; Cambridge witness language localized to Leicestershire; Cotton dialect unresolved; Digby collateral target language localized to north-west Gloucestershire
catalogSummary.title: Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten. X. Zu den ‘Signa ante Judicium’

catalogingSubject.label: Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten. X. Zu den ‘Signa ante Judicium’
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Hermann Varnhagen’s complete 1880 Anglia article as encoded in CME00100: two German prefatory paragraphs; a 398-line Cambridge Fifteen Signs work; a continuous Cotton sequence that modern DIMEV divides into a 56-line Invocation to the Creator and a 301-line Fifteen Signs work; German textual notes containing eight quoted Middle English comparison lines; the closing signature; and a German addendum. It is an edited selection containing three abstract medieval works, not one medieval poem, one manuscript witness, one digital encoding, or the complete contents of either manuscript.

contentParts.label: German editorial preface
notes: The preface discusses broader versions not transcribed in CME00100; those mentions are not treated as contained works.
contentParts.summary: Two German prose paragraphs survey prior Fifteen Signs scholarship, identify the Cambridge and Cotton witnesses, cite Stengel’s Digby fragment, and leave Cotton-versus-Digby priority unresolved.

contentParts.label: Cambridge Fifteen Signs: DIMEV 5309
notes: The four-line first LG is part of DIMEV 5309, not a separately indexed abstract work.
contentParts.summary: A 398-line Middle English couplet work beginning with a four-line invocation and narrating the fifteen signs before Doomsday.

contentParts.label: Cotton Invocation to the Creator: DIMEV 436
notes: DIMEV separates this work because it also circulates independently and in other witnesses; the XML’s 74-line LG does not mark the boundary after line 56.
contentParts.summary: A 56-line prayer in rhyming couplets praising God’s creation and redemption; the manuscript marginal title reads Quindecim signa and the text leads continuously into DIMEV 3000.

contentParts.label: Cotton Fifteen Signs with invocation: DIMEV 3000
notes: DIMEV reports a small paraph at the opening, Arabic day numbers, and a large yellow-washed final ‘Amen for charite’ at f. 91rb.
The Digby 86 text is a collateral witness and Stengel’s source, not the direct source of this Cotton transcription.
contentParts.summary: A 301-line Middle English couplet work: an eighteen-line invocation followed by 283 lines narrating the Fifteen Signs and concluding in prayer.

contentParts.label: Nine-couplet invocation
contentParts.summary: Eighteen lines invoke the king of heaven, ask protection from sin and fiends, and request grace before the signs narrative.

contentParts.label: Fifteen Signs narrative and closing prayer
contentParts.summary: The remaining 283 lines enumerate the signs before the Last Judgment and close with a plea for entry into bliss.

contentParts.label: German notes with Middle English comparison quotations

locators.value: two NOTE elements containing LG; 6 + 2 nested L

notes: These eight lines are not parts of DIMEV 5309, 436, or 3000; their exact quoted edition sources remain unresolved.
contentParts.summary: Two German notes quote six Cursor Mundi lines and two Hending lines as comparative evidence.

contentParts.label: Nachtrag zu s. 533
contentParts.summary: A short German addendum on printed p. 551 follows Varnhagen’s Greifswald signature.

notes: This dates Varnhagen’s edited selection, not composition of the medieval works.

assertion.notes: DIMEV 5309 has one witness. Its date is a terminus ante quem, not a composition date.

dateStatements.display: Mid-fifteenth century (the sole surviving DIMEV 5309 witness)

assertion.notes: This dates the direct source witness represented in CME00100, not necessarily the earliest surviving witness of either abstract work.

dateStatements.display: Cotton copies: second half of the fifteenth century (1450-1499)

assertion.notes: Digby is a collateral witness of the same DIMEV record and the source of Stengel’s comparison text; it is not the direct source of Varnhagen’s Cotton transcription and does not by itself date composition.

dateStatements.display: DIMEV 3000 is also attested in Digby 86, dated 1272-1282

assertion.notes: Witness dates and edition dates are not silently converted into composition dates.

extent.description: The complete XML has three prose P elements, six LG elements, and 763 L elements. The three base medieval works total 755 direct lines: Cambridge DIMEV 5309 has 398 lines; Cotton DIMEV 436 has 56; and Cotton DIMEV 3000 has 301. Two LGs nested in editorial notes add eight quoted lines, producing the representation total of 763. Nineteen PB elements cover printed pp. 533-551.

formStatements.description: The complete article combines German prose framing and notes with 755 direct Middle English verse lines and eight additional quoted verse lines.
All three indexed medieval works use rhyming couplets; the XML’s line-group boundaries do not always coincide with abstract-work boundaries.
German note prose encloses six quoted Cursor Mundi lines and two quoted Hending lines.

genres.label: Prayer
notes: The Cambridge work begins and ends with prayer; DIMEV 436 is an invocation; DIMEV 3000 has a nine-couplet invocation.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The eight quoted comparison lines are Middle English editorial evidence, not additional base-work lines.
The complete article is mixed German prose and Middle English verse; the three medieval component works themselves are verse.
The Cambridge and Cotton direct-source witnesses are distinct from Digby 86, which enters the article through Stengel’s printed comparison text.
Cambridge historical foliation 42v-45r and current foliation 50va-53ra are both retained rather than silently normalizing one away.
Cotton’s broad BL locus ff. 89r-91r and DIMEV’s exact columns ff. 89ra-91rb coexist; the XML 91v conflict remains explicit.
Manuscript holding and edition publication places are not promoted to medieval composition places.
The tags describe content actually encoded in CME00100, not every Fifteen Signs version or comparative work mentioned in Varnhagen’s preface and notes.

next_steps: Review current Fifteen Signs and invocation scholarship for composition ranges or earlier textual analogues.
Add part-scoped composition statements only where the evidence concerns the abstract work rather than one copy.
openQuestions.question: When were DIMEV 5309, 436, and 3000 originally composed, as distinct from their surviving witness dates?

next_steps: Compare all six DIMEV 3000 witnesses and modern critical scholarship.
Retain Varnhagen’s explicit uncertainty until a reliable stemmatic or version study resolves priority.
openQuestions.question: Is the Cotton DIMEV 3000 version an adaptation of the Digby-related version, or vice versa?

next_steps: Consult LALME/eLALME and manuscript-specific linguistic scholarship.
Do not transfer Digby’s north-west Gloucestershire target-language localization or Worcestershire production to Cotton.
openQuestions.question: What dialect or text-language localization can be assigned reliably to the Cotton copies of DIMEV 436 and 3000?

next_steps: Inspect current color images of ff. 89r-91v and compare foliation, columns, line endings, and adjacent texts.
Do not extend the cataloged Fifteen Signs into the Owayne Miles text beginning at f. 91v.
openQuestions.question: How should the XML/Varnhagen 91v milestone be reconciled with the BL catalog’s 91r ending and DIMEV’s precise 91rb ending?

next_steps: Resolve the abbreviated references ‘Curs. Mundi 22493 ff.’ and ‘Lond. text 176’ against Varnhagen’s bibliography and other Anglia 3 sections.
Keep the quotation lines cataloged as editorial evidence rather than base-work content until their source layers are confirmed.
openQuestions.question: Which exact editions or witnesses supplied Varnhagen’s six Cursor Mundi and two Hending quotation lines?

next_steps: Create independent work records when the corpus viewer and publication model can associate one encoded article with multiple separately publishable abstract works.
Until then, retain the explicit three-part model and related-work identifiers in CME00100.
openQuestions.question: Should the three contained DIMEV works eventually receive independent descriptive metadata records linked to the article by containment?

notes: Germany is the article’s publication context and Cambridge, London, and Oxford are holding locations; none is automatically a medieval composition place.
This is a target-hand linguistic localization, not a proven composition site or codex-production place.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English authors or compilers

assertion.notes: No named medieval author is supplied for any of the three DIMEV records. The collective display does not merge them into one authored work.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English authors or compilers

scope.description: selection and arrangement of the complete 1880 article

notes: This covers Varnhagen’s direct Cambridge and Cotton transcriptions. Stengel is the direct transcriber of the separately compared Digby text.

subjects.label: The Fifteen Signs before the Day of Judgment
Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment
Creation, cosmology, and the natural world

summaries.value: Hermann Varnhagen’s complete 1880 scholarly article edits two Middle English manuscript treatments of the Fifteen Signs before Judgment and frames them with German introduction, notes, and an addendum. The Cambridge section is one 398-line couplet work, DIMEV 5309. The Cotton section joins a 56-line creation prayer, DIMEV 436, to a distinct 301-line Fifteen Signs work, DIMEV 3000, whose first eighteen lines are an invocation. Varnhagen compares the Cotton signs with Stengel’s printed Digby 86 fragment but leaves their priority unresolved. Two notes additionally quote six lines from Cursor Mundi and two from Hending as comparative evidence.

A four-line invocation to the Holy Ghost and Jesus leads directly into a 394-line account of the fifteen signs preceding Doomsday, closing with a prayer for bliss. DIMEV treats all 398 lines as one work and records Ff.2.38 as its sole witness.

The Cotton section first praises God’s creation and redemption in 56 lines, then begins a separate 301-line Fifteen Signs work with a nine-couplet invocation, narrates the signs, and closes with prayer and ‘Amen for charite.’ A small manuscript paraph marks the second work, but Varnhagen and the XML serialize both inside one 74-line LG followed by a 283-line LG.

The three medieval works are related by subject and editorial juxtaposition but retain separate DIMEV identities. Physical manuscript witnesses, Varnhagen’s and Stengel’s editions, U-M’s encoding, the repository XML, modern catalogs, and digital facsimiles are lineage layers rather than alternate names for the cataloged article.

Eight XML L elements belong to two quotations embedded in Varnhagen’s German footnotes: six Cursor Mundi lines after Cotton base line 144 and two Hending lines after base line 190. They count toward the representation’s 763 total L elements but not the 755 base lines of the three medieval edited works.

titles.value: Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten. X. Zu den ‘Signa ante Judicium’

assertion.notes: A supplied English rendering for discovery; the German title remains preferred.

titles.value: On Middle English Poems. X. On the ‘Signs before Judgment’

notes: The XML and Varnhagen print the rubric; DIMEV records it at current f. 50va.
titles.value: Here foloweþ þe .XV. tokenys before the day of dome
The grace of the Holy Ghost
The grace of the holy goste, That ys ay stedfaste
That we may to the blysse wende, That lastyth ay wythowten ende
Almyȝty god, þat all hath wroght, Heven and helle and erþe of noght

notes: This is the abstract-work boundary inside the XML’s first 74-line Cotton LG.
titles.value: That we synnefull were fallen fro, Thorow Adam synne and Eve also
The Fifteen Signs before the Day of Judgment, prefaced by a short invocation of nine couplets
Kyng of heven, blessed þou be. Lorde of grace and of pyte
When we have done here our ende, To þy ryche blysse þat we mowe wende. Amen for charite!
